sections.markdown:
# CALL-NEXT-METHOD and NEXT-METHOD-P

Inside a method, `(call-next-method)` invokes the **next applicable method** for the same generic call — the next-most-specific primary if you're in a primary, or the next `:around` layer if you're in an `:around`.

With no arguments, the same arguments are passed through. With explicit arguments, you can change them — but the new values must still satisfy the specialisers, or you'll signal an error.

`(next-method-p)` returns true if there *is* a next method. Use it to guard `call-next-method` calls when you're not sure another method exists:

```lisp
(defmethod describe-it ((x dog))
  (cons :dog (when (next-method-p) (call-next-method))))
```

If you call `call-next-method` and there is no next method, it signals an error. So either know there is one, or check first.

This is how you build cooperative chains: a subclass method does its part, then asks the superclass method to do its part on top.

sections.code: (progn (defclass animal () ()) (defclass dog (animal) ()) (defgeneric tags (x)) (defmethod tags ((x animal)) (list :animal)) (defmethod tags ((x dog)) (cons :dog (call-next-method))) (tags (make-instance 'dog)))
sections.explain: DOG's method prepends :DOG, then chains to ANIMAL's method which returns (:ANIMAL).
NEXT-METHOD-P guarded the call; here there is no next method so we just return (list :animal).

sections.code: (progn (defclass a () ()) (defclass b (a) ()) (defclass c (b) ()) (defgeneric stack (x)) (defmethod stack ((x a)) '(:a)) (defmethod stack ((x b)) (cons :b (call-next-method))) (defmethod stack ((x c)) (cons :c (call-next-method))) (stack (make-instance 'c)))
sections.explain: C's method chains to B's, which chains to A's. Each prepends its tag.

sections.prompt: Define classes ITEM, FOOD (extends ITEM), APPLE (extends FOOD). Define generic function CATEGORIES returning a list. The ITEM method returns (:item); FOOD prepends :food via call-next-method; APPLE prepends :apple via call-next-method.
